essence: David tours Tony O'Connell's Chiang Mai Fetch.ai agent-swarm startup (pheromone-trail task marketplace, token economics, agent-launch.ai) and Tony offers to host the Chiang Mai AI community for free/cheap in his underused AI-research-lab building.

actions.text: Connect Ian and Steve with Tony about free/low-cost AI community space in his building
Get agent-launch.ai link and instructions from Tony's team to connect David's own agent

essence: David frames an assistant conversation whose sole goal is iteratively crafting a best-practice prompt (clear context, structure, step-by-step shaping).

David brokers an introduction between Guy, a Chiang Mai vocal/presentation coach, and Ian, a venture capitalist needing coaching before a Thursday Skype presentation, while demoing his OMI wearable capture — which Guy wants to buy (Ian sells them).

actions.text: Send Guy the link to the OMI wearable transcription device once Ian responds to the referral message

essence: David specs a SupportSignal UX experiment: formal database.schema.json of the incident wizard workflow (steps, questions, async predicates, categorization/observation fields), a tool mapping real exported data into a refreshable fake database.json, and 10 requirement-driven generated websites — toward an all-in-one prompt that regenerates visualizations as Angela's workflow evolves, with future human-in-the-loop override preserving AI values.

actions.text: Build UX folder via background agents: database/ (json + schema), data-mapping tool, requirements/ (10 specs), index.html + 10 generated visualization sites

essence: Lisa keeps miscounting broken links (~150–155) and ignores the dedicated system defining which links should and shouldn't be counted, even after a context reload — David wants to know why the system failed.

David commits to a 1–3 day quality campaign: migration-first old-suite-to-Jest test rewrite running both suites in parallel (like his prior convex s5/s8 setup), an audit log of easy wins, cross-component consistency checks as a quality red flag, and a documented stamp-in-time baseline commit with state management that survives context-window resets.

actions.text: Establish and document a stamp-in-time baseline commit enabling rollback and commit retrospectives before the test-migration campaign

essence: David directs the audit: leave legacy convex s5/s8 scripts alone, model a new s9 web test on s4 and start.sh patterns, commit everything to establish a baseline, and manually assess the ~150 broken links for quick wins while challenging Lisa's conflicting 340 count and asking whether state survives a session restart.

actions.text: Create s9 web test following s4 and start.sh script patterns (don't touch legacy s5/s8)
Commit baseline, then assess ~150 broken links individually and fix quick wins

essence: David approves phase-one scope ('just do the 48'), pushes for the voice/ElevenLabs mistakes to be captured as learnings and handled via the ElevenLabs skill, and insists commented-out links producing link-checker false negatives get fixed rather than just reported.

actions.text: Capture voice/ElevenLabs mistake learnings and confirm config changes go through the ElevenLabs skill
Remove commented-out links causing link-checker false negatives, then continue with remaining easy fixes

essence: David rejects an outdated voice-agent model comparison — GPT-4o nearing deprecation, Sonnet further along than listed, Haiku and open-source models (Qwen/Kimi) viable for tool calling — and demands OpenRouter-based, web-searched model selection instead of from-memory LLM answers.

David flags that the S9 test suite doesn't even start — he expected a runnable baseline he could watch grow instead of endless analysis — and orders a background investigation into whether 'meta archive' folders are actual archives or KDD how-to-archive instructions.

actions.text: Background task: determine whether meta-archive folders are archives or archiving instructions (naming-convention question)

essence: David tightens the validation workflow — delete the intermediate broken-links file and write results directly into the conversation, admit uncertainty instead of guessing, don't patch inherently broken setups — and demands session learnings (haiku vs open-source comparison, librarian QC-bot concept) be documented before the 7%-context handover, while questioning how archive template placeholders and anti-pattern example links should be classified.

actions.text: Document session learnings (haiku-vs-OSS comparison, librarian QC bot concept) plus a handover before context runs out
Rerun broken-links validation writing output directly into the conversation folder (no hidden files)

essence: David decides archive files can't be blindly excluded from link validation (they document HOW to archive, not archived data) and picks a RuboCop-style standardized ignore-comment pattern the Python tool can auto-skip; he also asks how to point the ElevenLabs setup at a new model and where the API key gets updated.

actions.text: Adopt RuboCop-style ignore-comment pattern so the Python link-validation tool auto-skips marked items
Add task to review all archive-related files before excluding them

essence: David extends broken-link remediation to FliHub — list projects, print broken links directly in-conversation (no hidden files), document the remediation pattern so librarians can reuse it, and run a background task sampling 20 'file does not exist' links to produce a delete-reference vs update-reference mapping, plus test FliHub server connectivity for a possibly deprecated endpoint.

actions.text: Document the broken-link remediation pattern for librarian reuse and propagate it to the documentation system
Background task: verify 20 file-not-exist links and produce delete-vs-update reference mapping with affected source files
Test FliHub server endpoint connectivity (possible deprecated endpoint behind 404 failures)
